chosen_title: IBM has unveiled chip technology that could help extend Moore's Law another decade
label: AI
headline: 100 Billion Transistors. One Fingernail.
image_prompt: A single microchip prototype no larger than a human thumbnail, held upright in polished stainless-steel lab tweezers against a deep-black cleanroom void. The chip face reveals an impossibly fine silver-blue lattice of circuit traces, their density blurring into abstraction at the edges, hinting at geometry smaller than individual atoms. Faint cool-light particles drift suspended in the near-field background, suggesting an invisible world of scale beyond human sight. The lower third of the frame is anchored in deep shadow where a gloved hand grips the tweezers. A bright accent glow radiates from directly beneath the chip's surface, casting a focused cold-white upward beam through the lattice like a sliver of compressed starlight.
tweets:
IBM just squeezed 100 billion transistors onto a chip the size of a fingernail.

Sub-1 nanometer. Twice the density of anything they built before.

This could extend Moore's Law by another decade. 🧵

For context: Moore's Law is the 1965 prediction by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore that transistor counts double roughly every 2 years — making chips faster and cheaper. Experts have declared it dead many times. It keeps surviving.

Why this IBM prototype is different:
• ~100B transistors — 2× the density of IBM's 2021 benchmark
• Sub-1nm process node — transistors only a few silicon atoms across
• Fingernail-sized die — more compute per mm² than anything before

To put sub-1nm in perspective: a human hair is ~80,000 nanometers wide. A silicon atom is about 0.2nm. IBM is now building transistors so small they're only a handful of atoms across — a scale where quantum physics, not classical engineering, runs the show.

Why it matters beyond the lab: every AI model you use runs on chips. Denser chips mean more AI compute with less energy. The race between IBM, TSMC, and Intel to reach sub-1nm will define who leads computing — and AI — for the next decade.

We've been hearing about the "death of Moore's Law" for 20 years. Every time the wall looked immovable, someone moved it. IBM just moved it again.
Follow @decodededge
Source: MIT Technology Review
